description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Central?

Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

description: When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Certificates?

Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

description: Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Classifiers?
Feature Caveat: Classifiers - Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

description: For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for Classifiers in HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.18.0001?
For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

description: The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?

Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.

description: No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for CMF?
CMF - No other checkpoint besides 'startup-configuration' gets migrated during the upgrade process.

feature: DHCP Server, DHCP Relay, and DHCP Snooping
description: DHCP Relay and DHCP Snooping can co-exist on the same switch. DHCP Snooping and DHCP Server cannot co-exist on the same switch. DHCP Snooping, DHCP Relay, and DHCP Server together cannot co-exist on the same switch.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for DHCP Server, DHCP Relay, and DHCP Snooping?

Feature Caveat: DHCP Server, DHCP Relay, and DHCP Snooping - DHCP Relay and DHCP Snooping can co-exist on the same switch. DHCP Snooping and DHCP Server cannot co-exist on the same switch. DHCP Snooping, DHCP Relay, and DHCP Server together cannot co-exist on the same switch.

description: When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Hot Patch?

Feature Caveat: Hot Patch - When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

description: Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for IP-SLA?

Feature Caveat: IP-SLA - Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

description: The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for IP Source Lockdown?

Feature Caveat: IP Source Lockdown - The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

description: Passing multicast traffic over GRE, 6in4 or 6in6 tunnels is not supported.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Multicast?
Feature Caveat: Multicast - Passing multicast traffic over GRE, 6in4 or 6in6 tunnels is not supported.

description: Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what RADIUS caveat is documented?
Feature Caveat: RADIUS - Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.

description: REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for REST?
REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

description: When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what is the caveat related to SNMP?

Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

description: DHCP Snooping (v4/v6) should not be enabled on the UBT client VLANs in either vlan extend and local vlan mode.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for UBT/DHCP-Snooping?
Feature Caveat: UBT/DHCP-Snooping - DHCP Snooping (v4/v6) should not be enabled on the UBT client VLANs in either vlan extend and local vlan mode.

description: Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

description: If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

description: Overlay/system partition information is included in HOST-RESOURCES-MIB polling. Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

messages.content: For 4100i AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for SNMP?

Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

description: Symptom: Dual-port AP uplinks connected via a LAG intermittently show member interfaces as “Blocked by Port Access Security.” the LAG stops forwarding and the end device cannot onboard until the interfaces are toggled. Scenario: This issue may occur when port-access device-profile is enabled on the LAG with mode block-until-profile-applied, using LLDP for profiling. When the LAG member that had the LLDP neighbor goes Layer-1 down, subsequent authentication fails and the interface becomes blocked.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

description: Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

description: Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

description: Symptom: Dual-port AP uplinks connected via a LAG intermittently show member interfaces as “Blocked by Port Access Security.” the LAG stops forwarding and the end device cannot onboard until the interfaces are toggled. Scenario: This issue may occur when port-access device-profile is enabled on the LAG with mode block-until-profile-applied, using LLDP for profiling. When the LAG member that had the LLDP neighbor goes Layer-1 down, subsequent authentication fails and the interface becomes blocked.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

description: Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

description: Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.
